primitives.name: Natural Numbers
primitives.formalStatement: The natural numbers N = {1, 2, 3, ...} satisfy the Peano axioms: there exists a first element 1, every element n has a unique successor S(n), no two elements share a successor, and the induction principle holds.
applicabilityPatterns: counting objects or elements in a set
establishing the basis for mathematical induction

primitives.name: Real Line Completeness
primitives.formalStatement: Every non-empty subset of R that is bounded above has a least upper bound (supremum). Equivalently, there are no gaps in the real number line.
prerequisites: Understanding of real numbers and ordering
applicabilityPatterns: proving existence of limits or suprema
establishing that a convergent sequence has a real limit

primitives.name: Integers
primitives.formalStatement: The integers Z = {..., -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, ...} extend the natural numbers to include zero and additive inverses, forming an ordered ring under addition and multiplication.
primitives.computationalForm: For any natural number n, its additive inverse -n satisfies n + (-n) = 0.

dependencies.description: Integers extend natural numbers by adding zero and negative numbers

compositionRules.yields: Complete additive group with identity and inverses
conditions: When subtraction must always be defined
compositionRules.example: Extending counting numbers to allow 3 - 5 = -2

applicabilityPatterns: representing signed quantities like temperature or debt
problems involving subtraction that may go below zero

primitives.name: Rational Numbers
primitives.formalStatement: The rational numbers Q = {p/q : p, q in Z, q != 0} form an ordered field under addition and multiplication, with equivalence p/q = r/s iff ps = qr.
primitives.computationalForm: Arithmetic: a/b + c/d = (ad + bc)/(bd); (a/b)(c/d) = ac/(bd); (a/b)^(-1) = b/a for a != 0.

dependencies.description: Rational numbers are ratios of integers

compositionRules.yields: A number system where division is always defined (except by zero)
conditions: When division must produce exact results
compositionRules.example: Dividing 1 by 3 to get the exact fraction 1/3

applicabilityPatterns: representing exact ratios like musical intervals
solving equations where results are fractional

primitives.name: Irrational Numbers
primitives.formalStatement: An irrational number is a real number that cannot be expressed as a ratio p/q of integers. The set of irrationals is R \ Q. Examples include sqrt(2), pi, and e.
primitives.computationalForm: Proof by contradiction: assume sqrt(2) = p/q in lowest terms, then 2q^2 = p^2 implies p is even, contradicting lowest terms.

dependencies.description: Irrational numbers are defined as those that are NOT rational

compositionRules.yields: The complete real number line without gaps
conditions: When both rational and irrational numbers are needed to fill the line
compositionRules.example: sqrt(2) lies between 1.414 and 1.415 on the number line, filling a gap the rationals leave

applicabilityPatterns: proving that certain numbers like sqrt(2) or pi cannot be exactly expressed as fractions
understanding why the real line has no gaps

primitives.name: Real Numbers
primitives.formalStatement: The real numbers R form a complete ordered field: closed under +, -, *, /, ordered by <, and satisfying the completeness axiom. R = Q union (R \ Q).
primitives.computationalForm: Real arithmetic follows field axioms: associativity, commutativity, distributivity, identities (0,1), inverses. Ordering: trichotomy, transitivity.

dependencies.description: Real numbers include all rationals
Real numbers include all irrationals
Completeness is the defining property that distinguishes R from Q

compositionRules.yields: A number system where limits of convergent sequences always exist
conditions: When analysis requires limiting processes
compositionRules.example: The sequence 1, 1.4, 1.41, 1.414, ... converges to sqrt(2) in R

applicabilityPatterns: any calculation involving continuous quantities
measuring distances, areas, or physical quantities

primitives.name: Complex Numbers
primitives.formalStatement: The complex numbers C = {a + bi : a, b in R, i^2 = -1} form an algebraically closed field. Every complex number has modulus |z| = sqrt(a^2 + b^2) and argument arg(z) = atan2(b, a).
primitives.computationalForm: Addition: (a+bi) + (c+di) = (a+c) + (b+d)i. Multiplication: (a+bi)(c+di) = (ac-bd) + (ad+bc)i. Modulus: |z| = sqrt(a^2+b^2).

dependencies.description: Complex numbers extend real numbers with the imaginary unit

compositionRules.yields: Polar form of complex numbers: z = r*e^(i*theta)
conditions: When phase and magnitude need separate representation
compositionRules.example: 1+i = sqrt(2) * e^(i*pi/4) in polar form

applicabilityPatterns: representing quantities with both magnitude and phase
solving polynomial equations that have no real roots

primitives.name: Absolute Value
primitives.formalStatement: For x in R, the absolute value |x| = x if x >= 0, |x| = -x if x < 0. Equivalently, |x| = sqrt(x^2). It measures the distance from x to 0 on the number line.
primitives.computationalForm: |x| = sqrt(x^2). Properties: |xy| = |x||y|, |x+y| <= |x|+|y| (triangle inequality), |x| >= 0 with equality iff x = 0.

dependencies.description: Absolute value is defined on real numbers

compositionRules.yields: Distance between two points on the line: |a - b|
conditions: When measuring separation between two real values
compositionRules.example: Distance between -3 and 5 is |(-3) - 5| = 8

applicabilityPatterns: measuring distance from zero or between two numbers
bounding the size of a quantity regardless of sign

primitives.name: Number Line
primitives.formalStatement: The number line is a one-to-one correspondence between the real numbers R and points on a line, preserving order and distance. Each point corresponds to exactly one real number and vice versa.
primitives.computationalForm: Map x in R to point P at signed distance x from origin O. Distance between points at a and b is |a - b|.

dependencies.description: The number line is the geometric representation of the real numbers

compositionRules.yields: Metric space structure on R with distance d(a,b) = |a-b|
conditions: When geometric distance on the real line is needed
compositionRules.example: Representing temperature on a thermometer as a point on the number line

applicabilityPatterns: visualizing ordering and distance relationships between numbers
geometric interpretation of inequalities

primitives.name: Density of Rationals
primitives.formalStatement: Between any two distinct real numbers a < b, there exists a rational number q such that a < q < b. Equivalently, Q is dense in R.
primitives.computationalForm: Given a < b, choose n such that 1/n < b - a (Archimedean property), then find integer m such that a < m/n < b.

dependencies.description: Density is a property of rationals within the reals
The theorem is about the rational numbers

compositionRules.yields: Understanding that Q is dense but not complete — R fills the gaps
conditions: When understanding the relationship between Q and R
compositionRules.example: Between any two irrationals there is a rational, yet between any two rationals there is an irrational

applicabilityPatterns: approximating any real number by a rational number
constructing sequences of rationals converging to a real limit

primitives.name: Triangle Inequality for Absolute Value
primitives.formalStatement: For all real numbers a, b: |a + b| <= |a| + |b|. Equality holds if and only if a and b have the same sign (or one is zero).

dependencies.description: The triangle inequality is a fundamental property of absolute value

compositionRules.yields: Metric space axiom: d(a,c) <= d(a,b) + d(b,c)
conditions: When proving metric space properties
compositionRules.example: Distance from A to C via B is at least as long as the direct path

applicabilityPatterns: bounding the magnitude of a sum by the sum of magnitudes
proving convergence of sequences using epsilon-delta arguments

primitives.name: Unit Circle
primitives.formalStatement: The unit circle is the set of points (x, y) in R^2 satisfying x^2 + y^2 = 1. Every point on the unit circle can be written as (cos(theta), sin(theta)) for a unique angle theta in [0, 2*pi).
primitives.computationalForm: Parameterization: x = cos(theta), y = sin(theta). Arc length from (1,0) to angle theta equals theta (in radians).

dependencies.description: Coordinates on the unit circle are real numbers

compositionRules.yields: Complex numbers of modulus 1: z = e^(i*theta) on the unit circle
conditions: When representing rotations or phases as complex numbers
compositionRules.example: The point (cos(pi/4), sin(pi/4)) corresponds to e^(i*pi/4) = (1+i)/sqrt(2)

applicabilityPatterns: defining trigonometric functions geometrically
representing angles and rotations in the plane

primitives.name: Radian Measure
primitives.formalStatement: One radian is the angle subtended at the center of a circle by an arc equal in length to the radius. A full circle is 2*pi radians. Conversion: theta_rad = theta_deg * pi/180.
primitives.computationalForm: Arc length s = r * theta (in radians). Full circle: 2*pi rad = 360 degrees. pi rad = 180 degrees.

dependencies.description: Radian measure is most naturally defined on the unit circle where arc length equals angle

compositionRules.yields: Natural angle measurement where arc length equals angle on unit circle
conditions: When angles must be compatible with calculus (derivatives of trig functions)
compositionRules.example: At radius 1, an arc of length pi/2 subtends a right angle

applicabilityPatterns: converting between degrees and radians
computing arc length from angle and radius

primitives.name: Sine Function
primitives.formalStatement: The sine function sin: R -> [-1,1] is defined as the y-coordinate of the point on the unit circle at angle theta from the positive x-axis. It is periodic with period 2*pi, odd: sin(-theta) = -sin(theta).
primitives.computationalForm: sin(0) = 0, sin(pi/6) = 1/2, sin(pi/4) = sqrt(2)/2, sin(pi/3) = sqrt(3)/2, sin(pi/2) = 1. Taylor: sin(x) = x - x^3/3! + x^5/5! - ...

dependencies.description: Sine is defined as the y-coordinate on the unit circle
Sine takes angles in radians as input

compositionRules.yields: Complete unit circle parameterization: (cos(theta), sin(theta))
conditions: When both coordinates of a point on the unit circle are needed
compositionRules.example: A point at angle pi/3 is (cos(pi/3), sin(pi/3)) = (1/2, sqrt(3)/2)

applicabilityPatterns: computing vertical components of circular or oscillatory motion
modeling periodic phenomena like waves and vibrations

primitives.name: Cosine Function
primitives.formalStatement: The cosine function cos: R -> [-1,1] is defined as the x-coordinate of the point on the unit circle at angle theta. It is periodic with period 2*pi, even: cos(-theta) = cos(theta). cos(theta) = sin(theta + pi/2).

dependencies.description: Cosine is defined as the x-coordinate on the unit circle
Cosine takes angles in radians as input

compositionRules.yields: Complete unit circle parameterization and Pythagorean identity
conditions: When decomposing a vector or oscillation into orthogonal components
compositionRules.example: Resolving a force F at angle theta into components F*cos(theta) and F*sin(theta)

applicabilityPatterns: computing horizontal components of circular or oscillatory motion
finding phase relationships between periodic signals

primitives.name: Tangent Function
primitives.formalStatement: The tangent function tan(theta) = sin(theta)/cos(theta), defined for all theta where cos(theta) != 0. It has period pi and vertical asymptotes at theta = pi/2 + n*pi.
primitives.computationalForm: tan(0) = 0, tan(pi/4) = 1, tan(pi/3) = sqrt(3). Derivative: d/dx tan(x) = sec^2(x) = 1 + tan^2(x).

dependencies.description: Tangent is the ratio of sine to cosine
Tangent is the ratio of sine to cosine

compositionRules.yields: The identity 1 + tan^2(theta) = sec^2(theta)
conditions: When relating tangent to secant in integration or identities
compositionRules.example: Simplifying integral of sec^2(x) dx = tan(x) + C

applicabilityPatterns: computing the slope of a line at an angle theta
relating opposite and adjacent sides in a right triangle

primitives.name: Amplitude
primitives.formalStatement: The amplitude A of a sinusoidal function y = A*sin(theta + phi) is the maximum displacement from the mean position. A >= 0, and |y| <= A for all theta.
primitives.computationalForm: For y = A*sin(omega*t + phi): amplitude = A, maximum value = A, minimum value = -A, peak-to-peak = 2A.

dependencies.description: Amplitude scales the sine function

compositionRules.yields: Complete sinusoidal description: y = A*sin(theta + phi)
conditions: When specifying the full shape of a sinusoidal signal
compositionRules.example: A sound wave with amplitude 0.1 Pa and phase shift pi/4

applicabilityPatterns: measuring the strength or intensity of an oscillation
determining the peak displacement of a wave

primitives.name: Phase
primitives.formalStatement: The phase phi of a sinusoidal function y = A*sin(omega*t + phi) is the angular offset that determines where in the cycle the function begins at t = 0. Phase is measured in radians.
primitives.computationalForm: Phase shift in time: delta_t = phi/omega. Two signals are in phase when phi_1 = phi_2, out of phase by pi when they differ by pi radians.

dependencies.description: Phase is an angular offset applied to the sine function
Phase is measured in radians

compositionRules.yields: Complete sinusoidal specification: A*sin(omega*t + phi)
conditions: When describing the initial state and shape of an oscillation
compositionRules.example: A pendulum released from maximum displacement has phase phi = pi/2

applicabilityPatterns: describing the timing offset between two periodic signals
determining when a periodic process starts within its cycle

primitives.name: Pythagorean Identity
primitives.formalStatement: For all theta in R: sin^2(theta) + cos^2(theta) = 1. This follows directly from the unit circle definition: the point (cos(theta), sin(theta)) lies on x^2 + y^2 = 1.
primitives.computationalForm: sin^2(theta) = 1 - cos^2(theta); cos^2(theta) = 1 - sin^2(theta). Used to convert between sin and cos in expressions.

dependencies.description: Identity involves sine
Identity involves cosine
The identity follows from the unit circle equation

compositionRules.yields: Connection between algebraic identity and geometric theorem
conditions: When the same a^2 + b^2 = c^2 pattern appears in both trig and geometry
compositionRules.example: In a right triangle with hypotenuse 1: (opposite)^2 + (adjacent)^2 = 1

applicabilityPatterns: simplifying expressions involving sin^2 and cos^2
converting between sine and cosine in trigonometric equations

primitives.name: Double Angle Formulas
primitives.formalStatement: sin(2*theta) = 2*sin(theta)*cos(theta). cos(2*theta) = cos^2(theta) - sin^2(theta) = 2*cos^2(theta) - 1 = 1 - 2*sin^2(theta).
primitives.computationalForm: Half-angle: sin^2(theta) = (1 - cos(2*theta))/2, cos^2(theta) = (1 + cos(2*theta))/2. These simplify integration of sin^2 and cos^2.

dependencies.description: Double angle formula for sine
Double angle formula for cosine
Alternative forms derive from sin^2 + cos^2 = 1

compositionRules.yields: Power-reduction formulas for integration
conditions: When integrating even powers of sin or cos
compositionRules.example: integral sin^2(x)dx = integral (1-cos(2x))/2 dx = x/2 - sin(2x)/4 + C

applicabilityPatterns: reducing powers of trigonometric functions in integrals
relating frequencies: converting between single and double frequency expressions

primitives.name: Euler's Formula
primitives.formalStatement: For all theta in R: e^(i*theta) = cos(theta) + i*sin(theta). The special case theta = pi gives Euler's identity: e^(i*pi) + 1 = 0.
primitives.computationalForm: cos(theta) = Re(e^(i*theta)) = (e^(i*theta) + e^(-i*theta))/2. sin(theta) = Im(e^(i*theta)) = (e^(i*theta) - e^(-i*theta))/(2i).

dependencies.description: Euler's formula connects the exponential function with complex trigonometry
Sine appears in the formula
Cosine appears in the formula

compositionRules.yields: Every point on the unit circle as e^(i*theta), unifying algebra and geometry
conditions: When switching between trigonometric and exponential representations
compositionRules.example: e^(i*pi/3) = cos(pi/3) + i*sin(pi/3) = 1/2 + i*sqrt(3)/2

applicabilityPatterns: converting between trigonometric and exponential forms of complex numbers
simplifying products and powers of trigonometric expressions using exponentials

primitives.name: Periodicity of Trigonometric Functions
primitives.formalStatement: sin(theta + 2*pi) = sin(theta) and cos(theta + 2*pi) = cos(theta) for all theta. The fundamental period of sin and cos is 2*pi. The fundamental period of tan is pi.
primitives.computationalForm: For periodicity T: f(x + T) = f(x) for all x. sin/cos: T = 2*pi. tan: T = pi. Frequency f = 1/T, angular frequency omega = 2*pi/T.

dependencies.description: Periodicity is a fundamental property of sine
Periodicity is a fundamental property of cosine
Periodicity follows from the circular nature of the unit circle

compositionRules.yields: Understanding that periodicity arises from the circular motion returning to its starting point
conditions: When explaining why trigonometric functions repeat
compositionRules.example: After rotating 2*pi radians around the unit circle, you return to the same point

applicabilityPatterns: determining the repetition interval of a periodic signal
simplifying trigonometric expressions by reducing angles modulo the period

primitives.name: Pythagorean Theorem
primitives.formalStatement: In a right triangle with legs a and b and hypotenuse c: a^2 + b^2 = c^2. Conversely, if a^2 + b^2 = c^2 for a triangle with sides a, b, c, then the triangle is right-angled.
primitives.computationalForm: c = sqrt(a^2 + b^2). Given any two sides, find the third: a = sqrt(c^2 - b^2). Verify right angle: check a^2 + b^2 = c^2.

dependencies.description: Lengths are real numbers
The Pythagorean identity sin^2 + cos^2 = 1 is the unit circle case of the Pythagorean theorem

compositionRules.yields: Euclidean distance: d = sqrt((x2-x1)^2 + (y2-y1)^2)
conditions: When computing distance between two points in a plane
compositionRules.example: Distance from (1,2) to (4,6) = sqrt(9+16) = sqrt(25) = 5

applicabilityPatterns: computing the length of the hypotenuse in a right triangle
finding distance between two points in Euclidean space

primitives.name: Euclidean Distance Formula
primitives.formalStatement: The Euclidean distance between points P = (x1, y1) and Q = (x2, y2) in R^2 is d(P,Q) = sqrt((x2-x1)^2 + (y2-y1)^2). In R^n: d(P,Q) = sqrt(sum_i (q_i - p_i)^2).
primitives.computationalForm: d = sqrt((x2-x1)^2 + (y2-y1)^2). Satisfies metric axioms: d(P,Q) >= 0, d(P,Q) = 0 iff P=Q, d(P,Q) = d(Q,P), d(P,R) <= d(P,Q) + d(Q,R).

dependencies.description: The distance formula is a direct application of the Pythagorean theorem
Coordinates and distances are real numbers

compositionRules.yields: Unified distance metric: in 1D it reduces to |a-b|, in 2D it's the Pythagorean distance
conditions: When generalizing 1D distance to higher dimensions
compositionRules.example: In R^1: d(a,b) = sqrt((a-b)^2) = |a-b|

applicabilityPatterns: measuring how far apart two points are in a plane or higher-dimensional space
computing the length of a line segment given its endpoints

primitives.name: Inner Product (Dot Product)
primitives.formalStatement: The inner product (dot product) of vectors u = (u1,...,un) and v = (v1,...,vn) in R^n is u . v = sum_i u_i * v_i = |u||v|cos(theta), where theta is the angle between u and v.
primitives.computationalForm: u . v = u1*v1 + u2*v2 + ... + un*vn. |u| = sqrt(u.u). cos(theta) = (u.v)/(|u||v|). u perp v iff u.v = 0.

dependencies.description: Components are real numbers
The norm derived from inner product uses the Pythagorean theorem
The geometric form uses cosine of the angle between vectors

compositionRules.yields: Angle between vectors: theta = arccos(u.v / (|u||v|))
conditions: When finding the angle between two direction vectors
compositionRules.example: Angle between (1,0) and (1,1) = arccos(1/sqrt(2)) = pi/4

applicabilityPatterns: computing the angle between two vectors
finding the component of one vector along another direction

primitives.name: Orthogonality
primitives.formalStatement: Two vectors u and v are orthogonal (perpendicular) if and only if u . v = 0. An orthogonal set is a set of vectors that are pairwise orthogonal. An orthonormal set is orthogonal with each vector having unit length.
primitives.computationalForm: Test: u . v = 0 implies orthogonality. Orthonormal basis: {e1,...,en} where ei . ej = delta_ij (Kronecker delta).

dependencies.description: Orthogonality is defined via the inner product being zero

compositionRules.yields: Orthogonal decomposition: v = proj_u(v) + (v - proj_u(v)), where the two components are orthogonal
conditions: When decomposing a vector into parallel and perpendicular components
compositionRules.example: Decomposing velocity into components along and perpendicular to a slope

applicabilityPatterns: testing whether two directions are perpendicular
constructing coordinate systems where axes are mutually perpendicular

primitives.name: Norm (Magnitude)
primitives.formalStatement: The Euclidean norm (magnitude, length) of a vector v = (v1,...,vn) is ||v|| = sqrt(v . v) = sqrt(v1^2 + ... + vn^2). It satisfies: ||v|| >= 0, ||v|| = 0 iff v = 0, ||c*v|| = |c|*||v||, and the triangle inequality ||u+v|| <= ||u|| + ||v||.
primitives.computationalForm: ||v|| = sqrt(v1^2 + v2^2 + ... + vn^2). Unit vector: v_hat = v / ||v||. ||v||^2 = v . v.

dependencies.description: Norm is defined as the square root of the inner product with itself
Norm of (P-Q) equals distance from P to Q

compositionRules.yields: Normalized inner product (cosine similarity): cos(theta) = (u.v)/(||u|| ||v||)
conditions: When comparing directions independent of magnitude
compositionRules.example: Cosine similarity between document vectors in information retrieval

applicabilityPatterns: computing the length or magnitude of a vector
normalizing a vector to unit length

primitives.name: Projection
primitives.formalStatement: The scalar projection of v onto u is comp_u(v) = (v . u) / ||u||. The vector projection is proj_u(v) = ((v . u) / (u . u)) * u. The rejection (orthogonal component) is v - proj_u(v).
primitives.computationalForm: proj_u(v) = ((v.u)/(u.u)) * u. |comp_u(v)| = |v.u|/||u||. Verify: proj_u(v) . (v - proj_u(v)) = 0.

dependencies.description: Projection formula uses the inner product
Scalar projection divides by the norm
Projection produces orthogonal decomposition

compositionRules.yields: Orthogonal decomposition: v = proj_u(v) + (v - proj_u(v))
conditions: When decomposing a vector into components along and perpendicular to a direction
compositionRules.example: Work done by force F along displacement d: W = proj_d(F) * ||d|| = F.d

applicabilityPatterns: finding the component of one vector along another direction
computing work done by a force in the direction of displacement

primitives.name: Orthogonal Decomposition
primitives.formalStatement: Any vector v in R^n can be decomposed into the sum of its projection onto a subspace W and its component orthogonal to W: v = proj_W(v) + perp_W(v), where proj_W(v) in W and perp_W(v) in W^perp.
primitives.computationalForm: For orthogonal basis {u1,...,uk} of W: proj_W(v) = sum_i ((v.ui)/(ui.ui)) * ui. Residual: perp_W(v) = v - proj_W(v).

dependencies.description: Orthogonal decomposition is built from repeated projection
The decomposition produces orthogonal components

compositionRules.yields: Gram-Schmidt process: constructing orthonormal basis from arbitrary basis
conditions: When an orthogonal basis is needed from a non-orthogonal one
compositionRules.example: Gram-Schmidt: given {v1, v2}, produce orthogonal {u1, u2 = v2 - proj_{u1}(v2)}

applicabilityPatterns: breaking a signal into independent orthogonal components
removing the component of a vector along an unwanted direction

primitives.name: Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality
primitives.formalStatement: For all vectors u, v in an inner product space: |u . v| <= ||u|| * ||v||. Equality holds if and only if u and v are linearly dependent (parallel).
primitives.computationalForm: |u . v| <= ||u|| * ||v||. Equivalent: (sum u_i * v_i)^2 <= (sum u_i^2)(sum v_i^2). Verify equality: check if u = c*v for some scalar c.

dependencies.description: The inequality bounds the inner product
The bound involves norms of both vectors

compositionRules.yields: Triangle inequality for vectors: ||u+v|| <= ||u|| + ||v|| (follows from Cauchy-Schwarz)
conditions: When proving the triangle inequality in an inner product space

applicabilityPatterns: bounding the correlation between two quantities
proving that the cosine of an angle is between -1 and 1

primitives.name: Law of Cosines
primitives.formalStatement: In a triangle with sides a, b, c and angle C opposite side c: c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2*a*b*cos(C). When C = pi/2, this reduces to the Pythagorean theorem.
primitives.computationalForm: c = sqrt(a^2 + b^2 - 2*a*b*cos(C)). Solve for angle: cos(C) = (a^2 + b^2 - c^2)/(2*a*b).

dependencies.description: Law of cosines generalizes the Pythagorean theorem to non-right triangles
The correction term involves the cosine of the included angle
Cauchy-Schwarz ensures the cosine term is well-defined

compositionRules.yields: Vector form: ||u-v||^2 = ||u||^2 + ||v||^2 - 2(u.v), connecting dot product to geometric distance
conditions: When computing distance between vector endpoints

applicabilityPatterns: finding the third side of a triangle when two sides and the included angle are known
computing the angle between two sides of a triangle when all three sides are known

primitives.name: Reciprocal Trigonometric Functions
primitives.formalStatement: sec(theta) = 1/cos(theta), csc(theta) = 1/sin(theta), cot(theta) = 1/tan(theta) = cos(theta)/sin(theta). These are defined wherever their denominators are nonzero.
primitives.computationalForm: sec(0) = 1, csc(pi/2) = 1, cot(pi/4) = 1. Identities: 1 + tan^2 = sec^2, 1 + cot^2 = csc^2.

dependencies.description: csc is reciprocal of sine
sec is reciprocal of cosine
cot is reciprocal of tangent

compositionRules.yields: Extended Pythagorean identities: 1+tan^2=sec^2 and 1+cot^2=csc^2
conditions: When simplifying expressions with secant or cosecant
compositionRules.example: integral sec^2(x)dx = tan(x)+C follows from the identity

applicabilityPatterns: computing reciprocals of trig functions in calculus
simplifying expressions involving sec, csc, or cot

primitives.name: Inverse Trigonometric Functions
primitives.formalStatement: arcsin: [-1,1] -> [-pi/2, pi/2], arccos: [-1,1] -> [0, pi], arctan: R -> (-pi/2, pi/2). These are the inverses of the restricted trig functions.
primitives.computationalForm: arcsin(sin(x)) = x for x in [-pi/2,pi/2]. d/dx arcsin(x) = 1/sqrt(1-x^2). d/dx arctan(x) = 1/(1+x^2). d/dx arccos(x) = -1/sqrt(1-x^2).

dependencies.description: arcsin is the inverse of sine
arccos is the inverse of cosine
arctan is the inverse of tangent

compositionRules.yields: Angle between vectors: theta = arccos(u.v/(|u||v|))
conditions: When computing the angle between two vectors
compositionRules.example: Angle between (1,0) and (0,1) = arccos(0) = pi/2

applicabilityPatterns: finding angles from known trig ratios
computing the angle between vectors using arccos

primitives.name: Sum and Difference Formulas
primitives.formalStatement: sin(A+B) = sin(A)cos(B) + cos(A)sin(B). cos(A+B) = cos(A)cos(B) - sin(A)sin(B). tan(A+B) = (tan(A)+tan(B))/(1-tan(A)tan(B)).
primitives.computationalForm: sin(A-B) = sin(A)cos(B) - cos(A)sin(B). cos(A-B) = cos(A)cos(B) + sin(A)sin(B). These are the foundation for double-angle and half-angle formulas.

dependencies.description: Sum formulas express sin(A+B) in terms of sin and cos
Sum formulas express cos(A+B) in terms of sin and cos

compositionRules.yields: Double angle as special case: sin(2A) = sin(A+A) = 2sin(A)cos(A)
conditions: Setting B=A in sum formulas
compositionRules.example: cos(A+B) with A=B gives cos(2A) = cos^2(A) - sin^2(A)

applicabilityPatterns: computing exact values of trig functions at non-standard angles
deriving double-angle and product-to-sum identities

primitives.name: Exponential Function
primitives.formalStatement: The exponential function exp(x) = e^x where e = lim_{n->inf}(1+1/n)^n ~ 2.71828. Properties: e^(a+b) = e^a*e^b, e^0 = 1, d/dx e^x = e^x, e^x > 0 for all x.
primitives.computationalForm: e^x = sum_{n=0}^inf x^n/n! = 1 + x + x^2/2 + x^3/6 + ... Inverse: ln(e^x) = x. Growth: e^x > x^n for large x.

dependencies.description: Exponential is defined on real numbers

compositionRules.yields: Complex exponential: e^(a+bi) = e^a(cos(b)+i*sin(b))
conditions: When extending the exponential to complex arguments

applicabilityPatterns: modeling exponential growth or decay
computing compound interest or population dynamics

primitives.name: Natural Logarithm
primitives.formalStatement: The natural logarithm ln(x) = log_e(x) is the inverse of the exponential function: ln(e^x) = x, e^(ln(x)) = x for x > 0. ln: (0,inf) -> R is increasing and concave.
primitives.computationalForm: ln(1) = 0, ln(e) = 1. Properties: ln(ab) = ln(a)+ln(b), ln(a^n) = n*ln(a). d/dx ln(x) = 1/x. integral 1/x dx = ln|x|+C.

dependencies.description: Logarithm is the inverse of the exponential
Logarithm maps positive reals to R

compositionRules.yields: Inverse pair: ln and exp undo each other
conditions: When solving exponential or logarithmic equations
compositionRules.example: Solve e^x = 5: x = ln(5) ~ 1.609

applicabilityPatterns: solving exponential equations by taking logarithms
integrating 1/x to get ln|x|

primitives.name: Polar Coordinates
primitives.formalStatement: Polar coordinates (r, theta) represent a point in the plane by distance r from the origin and angle theta from the positive x-axis. Conversion: x = r*cos(theta), y = r*sin(theta), r = sqrt(x^2+y^2), theta = atan2(y,x).
primitives.computationalForm: x = r*cos(theta), y = r*sin(theta). r = sqrt(x^2+y^2). theta = atan2(y,x). Area element: dA = r*dr*d_theta.

dependencies.description: Polar coordinates use the unit circle parameterization
r is the Euclidean distance from origin

compositionRules.yields: Complex number in polar form: z = r*e^(i*theta)
conditions: When representing complex numbers geometrically
compositionRules.example: 3+4i in polar: r=5, theta=arctan(4/3), so z = 5*e^(i*arctan(4/3))

applicabilityPatterns: describing circular or spiral curves more naturally than Cartesian
converting between rectangular and polar representations

primitives.name: Set Notation and Operations
primitives.formalStatement: A set is a collection of distinct elements. Notation: {x : condition}. Operations: union A cup B, intersection A cap B, complement A^c, difference A  B. Subset: A subset B. Empty set: {}.
primitives.computationalForm: A cup B = {x : x in A or x in B}. A cap B = {x : x in A and x in B}. A  B = {x : x in A and x not in B}. |A cup B| = |A| + |B| - |A cap B|.

dependencies.description: Sets formalize the collections of numbers N, Z, Q, R, C

compositionRules.yields: Interval notation: (a,b), [a,b], [a,b) as subsets of R
conditions: When describing subsets of the real line
compositionRules.example: The domain of sqrt(x) is [0, infinity) = {x in R : x >= 0}

applicabilityPatterns: defining domains and ranges of functions
describing collections of numbers or solutions

primitives.name: Function
primitives.formalStatement: A function f: A -> B is a rule that assigns to each element x in domain A exactly one element f(x) in codomain B. The range (image) is {f(x) : x in A} subset B.
primitives.computationalForm: Evaluate: f(a) = output. Composition: (f circ g)(x) = f(g(x)). Inverse: f^{-1}(y) = x iff f(x) = y, exists iff f is bijective.

dependencies.description: Functions are defined between sets

compositionRules.yields: Real-valued functions f: R -> R, the primary objects of calculus
conditions: When studying functions from reals to reals
compositionRules.example: f(x) = x^2 maps R to [0, infinity)

applicabilityPatterns: defining mappings between sets with unique outputs
specifying the domain and range of a mathematical rule

primitives.name: Cartesian Coordinate Plane
primitives.formalStatement: The Cartesian coordinate plane R^2 = R x R identifies each point with an ordered pair (x, y). The x-axis is horizontal, y-axis is vertical, intersecting at the origin (0,0). Quadrants numbered I-IV counterclockwise.
primitives.computationalForm: Point: (x, y). Distance: d = sqrt((x2-x1)^2 + (y2-y1)^2). Midpoint: ((x1+x2)/2, (y1+y2)/2). Slope: m = (y2-y1)/(x2-x1).

dependencies.description: Coordinates are real numbers
Each axis is a number line

compositionRules.yields: Extension from 1D to 2D: two perpendicular number lines create the plane
conditions: When visualizing functions and geometric relationships
compositionRules.example: Plotting y = x^2: each x maps to a point (x, x^2) in the plane

applicabilityPatterns: plotting functions and curves in two dimensions
locating points using horizontal and vertical coordinates

primitives.name: Cross Product (Preview)
primitives.formalStatement: The cross product u x v of two vectors in R^3 produces a vector perpendicular to both: u x v = (u2*v3-u3*v2, u3*v1-u1*v3, u1*v2-u2*v1). |u x v| = |u||v|sin(theta).
primitives.computationalForm: u x v = det([i,j,k; u1,u2,u3; v1,v2,v3]). |u x v| = area of parallelogram. Direction: right-hand rule. u x v = -(v x u) (anti-commutative).

dependencies.description: Cross product complements the dot product for 3D vectors
Magnitude uses sine of angle between vectors

compositionRules.yields: Complete 3D vector algebra: dot product gives scalar (projection), cross product gives vector (perpendicular)
conditions: When working with 3D geometry and physics
compositionRules.example: Torque = r x F: perpendicular to both radius and force, magnitude = |r||F|sin(theta)

applicabilityPatterns: computing areas of parallelograms from edge vectors
finding a vector perpendicular to two given vectors in 3D

primitives.name: Vector
primitives.formalStatement: A vector in R^n is an ordered n-tuple v = (v_1, v_2, ..., v_n). Vectors have magnitude (norm) and direction. Addition: u+v = (u_1+v_1, ..., u_n+v_n). Scalar multiplication: c*v = (c*v_1, ..., c*v_n).
primitives.computationalForm: v = (v_1,...,v_n). |v| = sqrt(sum v_i^2). Unit vector: v/|v|. Zero vector: (0,...,0). Dimension: n components.

dependencies.description: Vector components are real numbers

compositionRules.yields: Geometric objects with both magnitude and direction, extending scalars
conditions: When a quantity has both size and direction
compositionRules.example: Velocity (3, 4) m/s: speed = 5 m/s, direction = arctan(4/3) from x-axis

applicabilityPatterns: representing quantities with both magnitude and direction
performing arithmetic on multi-dimensional data

primitives.name: Right Triangle Trigonometry
primitives.formalStatement: In a right triangle with angle theta: sin(theta) = opposite/hypotenuse, cos(theta) = adjacent/hypotenuse, tan(theta) = opposite/adjacent. These ratios are independent of triangle size (by similarity).
primitives.computationalForm: SOH-CAH-TOA. Given angle and one side, find all others. sin(30) = 1/2, cos(45) = sqrt(2)/2, tan(60) = sqrt(3).

dependencies.description: Right triangle ratios are the original definition of trig functions
The Pythagorean theorem relates the three sides

compositionRules.yields: Complete right triangle solver: given angle + side, find all measurements
conditions: When solving triangles for unknown sides or angles
compositionRules.example: Ladder 10m at 60 degrees: height = 10*sin(60) = 8.66m, base = 10*cos(60) = 5m

applicabilityPatterns: solving right triangles to find unknown sides or angles
computing heights, distances, and angles in practical surveying
